replies.author: @mattmurdock1180
replies.text:
@comixof2morrow You didn’t even explain how the new Superman is inaccurate, all you said was that his suit is baggy and that he got angry in that interview scene.

In fact, David's Superman shows more personality and aligns more closely with the comic book version than Henry Cavill’s interpretation. Cavill’s Superman often comes across as conflicted, emotionally distant, and burdened by his role, he struggles with whether or not to save people and is weighed down by guilt and isolation. That might work for some darker takes, but it doesn’t reflect the hopeful, morally grounded Superman from the comics.

David’s version, on the other hand, actually embodies Superman’s core values, compassion, responsibility, and moral clarity. His anger in the interview scene with Lois is completely justified, it wasn’t a public outburst or a meltdown in front of the media or the United Nations. It was a private, vulnerable moment with someone he trusts, Lois, the one person he can truly be himself with.

Lois questioned whether he should have interfered in the Boravian war, accusing him of representing the United States. Clark responded that he wasn’t representing any government, he was acting on his own conscience to do good. He even says loudly, “People were going to die,” expressing his frustration that human life isn’t being valued. His anger isn’t uncontrolled rage, it’s righteous and compassionate. He doesn’t want to be a government weapon or political pawn. He just wants to help people, everyone, because that’s what his parents taught him.

This is a Superman who was raised to care about every life, unlike Cavill’s version, whose parents told him things like “maybe you should have let them die” and “you don’t owe this world anything.” That philosophy runs counter to everything Superman represents in the comics.

David’s Superman cares. He believes in people. He acts. And that makes him far more comic accurate than a Superman who stands on the sidelines, uncertain if the world deserves to be saved.

replies.author: @mattmurdock1180
replies.text:
His anger in the interview scene with Lois is completely justified, it wasn’t a public outburst or a meltdown in front of the media or the United Nations. It was a private, vulnerable moment with someone he trusts, Lois, the one person he can truly be himself with.

Lois questioned whether he should have interfered in the Boravian war, accusing him of representing the United States. Clark responded that he wasn’t representing any government, he was acting on his own conscience to do good. He even says loudly, “People were going to die,” expressing his frustration that human life isn’t being valued. His anger isn’t uncontrolled rage, it’s righteous and compassionate. He doesn’t want to be a government weapon or political pawn. He just wants to help people, everyone, because that’s what his parents taught him.

This is a Superman who was raised to care about every life, unlike Cavill’s version, whose parents told him things like “maybe you should have let them die” and “you don’t owe this world anything.” That philosophy runs counter to everything Superman represents in the comics.

replies.author: @ChuckM0503
replies.text: ​@mattmurdock1180Shh! You're making too much sense for the Snyder simps.

replies.author: @vinceventresca6763
replies.text:
In Man of Steel, his parents acted like any real set of caring parents would: loving, protective, trying to shield him from the nastiness of the world as long as they can until he’s ready.

The “maybe” was a to show how quickly things have escalated, and how his dad is trying to fully comprehend what just happened.

He’d never teach Clark to let people die, but he fears for his son and how the world would react to him once he’s revealed, and that’s what led to “maybe”.

The pause afterwards has thrown everybody off, and they incorrectly use it in their arguments against Man of Steel.

replies.author: @mattmurdock1180
replies.text:
@vinceventresca6763 The issue isn’t that the Kents are scared or protective, that part’s understandable. The problem is how that fear is portrayed as wisdom rather than something Clark ultimately grows beyond.

Jonathan Kent telling Clark “maybe” he should’ve let a bus full of kids die isn’t just a moment of emotional panic, it’s presented as a serious moral dilemma. That’s not a pause of shock. That’s a deliberate writing choice that frames morality as negotiable, and it paints Jonathan as someone more concerned with secrecy than with doing the right thing. That fundamentally undercuts what he’s supposed to represent: a moral compass, not a caution sign.

Sure, real parents would be scared. But Jonathan Kent isn’t just a “real” dad, he’s the guy who helps raise Superman. He’s not perfect, but he’s supposed to instill in Clark the idea that doing good matters, even when it’s hard. Snyder turns that on its head. Instead of “you have a gift, and the world needs it,” we get “maybe the world doesn’t deserve it.” That’s not nuanced parenting, that’s fear masquerading as philosophy.

And this same bleak mindset infects Cavill’s Superman. In Batman v Superman, he says “no one stays good in this world.” That line alone is wildly out of character. Superman, at his core, is about hope, belief in humanity, and the idea that no one is beyond redemption. He knows there are bad people, but he chooses to see the best in them, because someone needs to. That’s what makes him Superman, not his powers, but his unshakable faith in the good.

Snyder's version strips that all away in favor of a moody, reluctant savior who doubts the very ideals Superman is supposed to stand for. So no, people aren’t misreading these lines, they’re pointing out that Snyder fundamentally misunderstood the character’s moral foundation.

replies.author: @davetheimpaler204
replies.text: Absolutely. I've been a comics fan all my life and was dreaming about superhero film universes in the 90's. When the MCU stuff finally happened, I was disappointed because (with few exceptions like Daredevil on Netflix) it never lived up to what it should have been. I liked DC stuff like Man Of Steel because it actually took the characters seriously as solemn, mythic icons in a modern pantheon instead of the MCU's bland "heckin' quirky Millennial chungus!" action-comedy BS. However, DC/WB got cold feet and were too cowardly to stay the course and instead wasted all their potential by trying to play "catch up" and ape Disney's MCU. Now we have a Superman movie that looks practically indistinguishable from an MCU film, right down to the overdesigned, goofy costumes, Millennial-pandering camp and overly maudlin sentimentality. I'm sure it will be an entertaining enough film, like most all of Gunn's stuff, but I'm just not very excited about it.

author: @Bootstrap670
text:
James Gunn’s Superman already feels off-track. The character looks too soft, too timid — more like a polite intern than the embodiment of strength, virtue, and unshakable presence. Superman is supposed to inspire awe, not blend in. This version seems to lean too far into vulnerability and emotional softness, stripping away the raw masculine energy that made the character iconic.

Superman isn’t just about kindness — he’s a symbol of power held in restraint, of a man who could dominate but chooses to protect. If that core is missing, all we’re left with is a cape and a costume, not a true Superman.

author: @larrywells5908
text: I just don't want us collectively as fans to be addicted to this negative news cycle. I for one would love to see a little more optimism. And I'm not saying we've had good reason to have any optimism. Especially with the way Disney has done Marvel fans, Star Wars fans not to mention Indiana Jones fans. That's not even talking about all the games that they've ruined and the crap we've been through. But I know like the mainstream media we can get stuck in this negative cycle and in it for the clicks mentality because negativity and anger gets more reactions I understand but I for one would love to see some optimism, but optimism that paid off. But I'm not delusional I know we have nothing to be hopeful for. I wish James gunn all the luck and success he can have on this project I am desperate to be excited for something again. That's what first brought me to sites like you guys and others
